answer: The sound /ɯ/ is present in underlying forms because it has a phonological effect - it triggers labialization (ʷ) on preceding consonants before deleting. This is evident in words like 'kewa' where /ɯ/ causes the /w/ to be labialized but then deletes itself. Linguists posit abstract segments like /ɯ/ when they cause systematic changes in surrounding sounds, even if they don't surface. This helps explain why certain consonants become labialized in the pronunciation when there's no visible trigger in the surface form.
